summary: Joel Spolsky announces Trello, a new product born from Fog Creek Software's effort to keep employees motivated by creating small teams to launch new products. The idea originated from Joel's frustration with never knowing what his team was working on. An early concept called 'Five Things' limited everyone to exactly five tasks, but evolved into Trello. Built by a small team following lean startup principles, Trello was dogfooded from just 700 lines of code and quickly proved to be an incredibly useful collaboration tool.
key_points: Fog Creek created four two-person teams to launch new products, motivated by employee retention and excitement rather than pure business strategy
The lean startup approach guided development: ship early, listen to customers, and pivot until you find product-market fit
The origin problem was simple: Joel never knew what anyone was working on, and half the time disagreed with their priorities
The 'Five Things' concept — limiting everyone to exactly five tasks — was a precursor that helped crystallize the idea behind Trello
Dogfooding from just 700 lines of code validated the product early and revealed they had a hit
money_quotes: If we want to keep our employees excited and motivated for another ten years, we were going to need some new things to work on.
After ten years in management I still never knew what anyone was supposed to be working on.
Once in a while I would walk around asking everyone what they were doing, and half the time, my reaction was 'why the hell are you working on THAT?'
Nobody was ever allowed to have SIX things assigned to them. If you have too many things on your to-do list, your motivation tends to sag.
It had to be super simple and friction-free so that everyone would use it, but it had to be powerful, too.
We started dogfooding the product when it was only 700 lines of code, and even in that super-simple form, we found it incredibly useful.
key_insight: Great products can emerge from solving your own mundane management frustrations — not knowing who's working on what — when you combine that irritation with lean development and a relentless focus on simplicity.
